title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a woman called Natalie talking about volunteering at a coastal aquarium. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a woman called Natalie talking about volunteering at a coastal aquarium. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: A woman called Natalie talks about volunteering at a coastal aquarium.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Natalie and I coordinate volunteers at Harbour Aquarium, which looks out over the sea on the west side of town. The aquarium is quite modern inside, although the building has had several uses. Visitors often assume it was built for fish displays, but it began life as a [9]boat warehouse[/9], where local fishermen stored equipment in winter. About fifteen years ago it was redesigned, with tanks installed on two floors and a small teaching room added at the back.||Our volunteers are not expected to clean large tanks or handle dangerous animals. Much of their role is helping visitors understand what they are looking at. At present, the display that creates the most questions is the one about [10]sea turtles[/10], because people want to know why plastic in the ocean is such a serious problem for them. New volunteers join an introductory morning before they begin. Instead of learning the names of every fish, they first practise speaking to visitors and receive training in [11]emergency procedures[/11], including what to do if someone slips near a tank or a child becomes separated from their family.||Everyone is given a choice of duties. Some people enjoy welcoming school parties at the entrance; others prefer quieter work. Those joining for the first time generally help prepare [12]activity sheets[/12] for children, placing them on tables and checking pencils are ready. Once they feel more confident, they may answer simple questions in the galleries. The aquarium supplies a uniform shirt, and volunteers may wear ordinary comfortable trousers. Because the floors can become wet, we insist on [13]non-slip shoes[/13]; trainers are acceptable only if their soles provide a good grip.||A lot of our conservation work happens away from public view. Injured animals occasionally arrive from beaches nearby. At the moment, our staff are caring for several young [14]seals[/14] that were found without their mothers after storms. Volunteers cannot feed them, but they may help record the food and equipment being used. We also collect information outside. On monthly beach visits, helpers record rubbish on a [15]survey form[/15], rather than simply putting everything into bags, since the information helps us identify where pollution comes from. The rubbish is, of course, removed afterwards.||We want volunteers to learn as well as help. Last year the most popular evening talk was about dolphins, but this year people booked places fastest for our session on [16]underwater photography[/16], which includes advice on taking clear pictures without disturbing wildlife. Anyone who volunteers for twenty hours is entitled to a free [17]family ticket[/17], so they can return with relatives and explore the aquarium as visitors. Finally, this summer’s fundraising event will not be our usual sponsored walk. Instead, we are holding a [18]beach concert[/18] near the harbour, with local musicians giving their time for free. Money raised will support our animal rescue work. I have application forms available if you are interested in joining us.

dialogue.speaker: natalie
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Natalie and I coordinate volunteers at Harbour Aquarium, which looks out over the sea on the west side of town. The aquarium is quite modern inside, although the building has had several uses. Visitors often assume it was built for fish displays, but it began life as a boat warehouse, where local fishermen stored equipment in winter. About fifteen years ago it was redesigned, with tanks installed on two floors and a small teaching room added at the back.||Our volunteers are not expected to clean large tanks or handle dangerous animals. Much of their role is helping visitors understand what they are looking at. At present, the display that creates the most questions is the one about sea turtles, because people want to know why plastic in the ocean is such a serious problem for them. New volunteers join an introductory morning before they begin. Instead of learning the names of every fish, they first practise speaking to visitors and receive training in emergency procedures, including what to do if someone slips near a tank or a child becomes separated from their family.||Everyone is given a choice of duties. Some people enjoy welcoming school parties at the entrance; others prefer quieter work. Those joining for the first time generally help prepare activity sheets for children, placing them on tables and checking pencils are ready. Once they feel more confident, they may answer simple questions in the galleries. The aquarium supplies a uniform shirt, and volunteers may wear ordinary comfortable trousers. Because the floors can become wet, we insist on non-slip shoes; trainers are acceptable only if their soles provide a good grip.||A lot of our conservation work happens away from public view. Injured animals occasionally arrive from beaches nearby. At the moment, our staff are caring for several young seals that were found without their mothers after storms. Volunteers cannot feed them, but they may help record the food and equipment being used. We also collect information outside. On monthly beach visits, helpers record rubbish on a survey form, rather than simply putting everything into bags, since the information helps us identify where pollution comes from. The rubbish is, of course, removed afterwards.||We want volunteers to learn as well as help. Last year the most popular evening talk was about dolphins, but this year people booked places fastest for our session on underwater photography, which includes advice on taking clear pictures without disturbing wildlife. Anyone who volunteers for twenty hours is entitled to a free family ticket, so they can return with relatives and explore the aquarium as visitors. Finally, this summer’s fundraising event will not be our usual sponsored walk. Instead, we are holding a beach concert near the harbour, with local musicians giving their time for free. Money raised will support our animal rescue work. I have application forms available if you are interested in joining us.

questions.question: The aquarium building was originally used as a (9) .
questions.explanation: Natalie explains that the building first served as a 'boat warehouse'.
